title: TubeTube: Perfectly Timed Videos for Your Journey
content: Are you ever bored and need something to do during your long ride on the DC Metro? TubeTube can help! Enter your start and stop stations, and TubeTube will calculate your trip length and recommend interesting videos that fit your travel time! This is a Ruby on Rails application. Try it out here, or view the Github repo here.

title: Basic Search Algorithm
content: I'm building a basic search algorithm in Python that allows a user to search an RSS feed from Craiglist. Click here to view the project repo on Github. I've also written a blog post exploring this project, the challenges I'm facing, and ideas I have for further exploration.

title: Calculator Race
content: This is simple game I built using Meteor JS. Two players can compete against one another to answer math questions the fastest. I used Meteor's MongoDB and Spacebars templating, as well as JQuery and Underscore JS for this project. Click here to view the project.

title: Head to Head! Game
content: The first portfolio project for my Web Development Immersive course. It's a guessing game where the players try to guess their friends preferences on random topics. I built the game logic with Ruby and routed actions using the Ruby Gem/framework, Sinatra on top of a Postgresql database. I also used Git and Github for version control and deployed to Heroku. You can see the game in action here, or view the Github repo here.

title: Drizzly Weather App
content: Collaborative project built in about 10 hours with a team of five people. This is a weather app that queries the Weather Underground API and makes dynamic AJAX calls to present conditions in the user's favorite locations. We used Rails and the Ruby Gem Devise to set up user authentication and basic HTTP routes. I contributed to setting up models, the Ruby Devise Gem, and decoding the API's data response into user-friendly views. You can view the Github repo here.

title: Tic Tac Toe...vs the Computer
content: The computer is not very smart yet, but it can prevent you from winning on the diagonals. In future versions, I will make the computer be able to block additional types of moves. It's eerie to see the game coming to life! You can change the size of the board on line 653, but keep the board a square larger than a 2x2 grid or else it will break. You can play the human-only version of the game here.

title: Blackjack
content: Blackjack! There are still a couple of bugs and this was built with a template that started with maybe 30% of the code already there, but it's still amazing to have made this.  There are a couple of bugs that need to be worked out, but it's mostly playable.  It feels really amazing to have built one of the old Windows card games.

title: Pong
content: This was a project for a Coursera  Class done in Python. Unfortunately, I lost the finished version, which kept score.  About 30% of the code was already provided in the template.
